detail:
The structural defect is real but the claimed impact is overstated. In deprovision_tenant (apps/orchestrator/services.py:691-770) the OpenRouter sub-key and KV secret are deleted FIRST (lines 705-733), each inside its own log-and-continue try/except so those failures never reach the outer handler. The outer except (766-770) is reachable only when container/file-share/identity teardown (736-743) raises; it sets status=SUSPENDED and re-raises but does NOT clear openrouter_key_hash / openrouter_key_secret_name (nor container_id) — those fields are cleared only on the success path (750-762). So a partial deprovision can leave a SUSPENDED tenant whose key_hash/secret_name still reference an OR sub-key + KV secret that were already deleted, and on resubscribe handle_checkout_completed (apps/billing/services.py:333-337) takes the SUSPENDED->ACTIVE wake path (should_provision=False, no key re-mint), so the stale references persist into the live tenant. The except-path field-clearing gap is genuine.

Two corrections to the impact narrative: (1) The billing/credit path that 'trusts' the hash — sync_or_key_limit (apps/billing/credits.py:42-76) — is gated on OPENROUTER_PER_TENANT_KEYS_ENABLED (default False, config/settings/base.py:450) and is explicitly best-effort: update_sub_key on the dead hash throws but is swallowed by the try/except at line 75 with only a warning. It does NOT silently break a chat turn; check_budget + record_usage remain the real enforcement. So 'billing/credit logic operates on a key that no longer exists' is technically true but harmless (logged no-op). (2) The dangling OR reference is a minor facet of a deeper, unnamed defect on the SAME path: container_id is also left set on a container that delete_container_app already destroyed, so reactivation's scale_container_app (services.py:361-366) targets a deleted container — a 'partial-deprovision-marked-SUSPENDED is corrupt and not cleanly resumable' problem that dominates the OR-key symptom.

Reachability is narrow: requires per-tenant OR keys enabled in prod AND an Azure teardown failure that lands after the (best-effort) OR/KV deletes but during container/identity teardown AND a later resubscribe of that same tenant. Real but rare, and the dominant failure on resume would be the deleted-container wake, not the OR key. Net: genuine low-severity data-integrity/consistency gap, not the medium silent-inference-failure described.
suggested_fix: In the except block (services.py:766-770), since the OR sub-key + KV secret are unconditionally deleted before any teardown that can raise, clear openrouter_key_hash and openrouter_key_secret_name (and ideally container_id/container_fqdn/managed_identity_id if their delete already succeeded) when marking the tenant SUSPENDED — or, more robustly, perform the field clears immediately after each successful resource delete rather than batched at the end, so partial-failure state always matches reality. Better still: do NOT revert a partially-torn-down tenant to SUSPENDED (a resumable state) — use a distinct DEPROVISION_FAILED/needs-attention status so a half-deleted tenant can't be silently woken on resubscribe.
evidence: apps/orchestrator/services.py:705-733 — OR sub-key + KV secret deleted first, each in its own swallowing try/except (failures never reach outer except)
apps/orchestrator/services.py:736-743 — container/file-share/identity teardown; only these (or the final save) can trip the outer except
apps/orchestrator/services.py:746-762 — key/container field clears live ONLY in the success path
apps/orchestrator/services.py:766-770 — except sets status=SUSPENDED + re-raise, no field clearing
apps/billing/services.py:333-337 — SUSPENDED->ACTIVE reactivation wakes existing container, should_provision=False, no key re-mint
apps/billing/credits.py:61-76 — sync_or_key_limit gated on flag + best-effort try/except: dead hash => warning, not a broken turn
